root_text.ud1.2:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā uruvelāyaṁ viharati najjā nerañjarāya tīre bodhirukkhamūle paṭhamābhisambuddho.
root_text.ud1.2:1.3: Tena kho pana samayena bhagavā sattāhaṁ ekapallaṅkena nisinno hoti vimuttisukhapaṭisaṁvedī.
root_text.ud1.2:1.4: Atha kho bhagavā tassa sattāhassa accayena tamhā samādhimhā vuṭṭhahitvā rattiyā majjhimaṁ yāmaṁ paṭiccasamuppādaṁ paṭilomaṁ sādhukaṁ manasākāsi:
root_text.ud1.2:2.1: “Iti imasmiṁ asati idaṁ na hoti, imassa nirodhā idaṁ nirujjhati, yadidaṁ—
root_text.ud1.2:3.1: Atha kho bhagavā etamatthaṁ viditvā tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imaṁ udānaṁ udānesi:

translation_text.ud1.2:1.2: At one time, when he was first awakened, the Buddha was staying in Uruvelā at the root of the tree of awakening on the bank of the Nerañjarā River.
translation_text.ud1.2:1.3: There the Buddha sat cross-legged for seven days without moving, experiencing the bliss of freedom.
translation_text.ud1.2:1.4: When seven days had passed, the Buddha emerged from that state of immersion. In the second part of the night, he carefully applied the mind to dependent origination in reverse order:
translation_text.ud1.2:2.1: “When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases. That is:
translation_text.ud1.2:2.4: When consciousness ceases, name and form cease.
translation_text.ud1.2:2.5: When name and form cease, the six sense fields cease.
translation_text.ud1.2:2.6: When the six sense fields cease, contact ceases.
translation_text.ud1.2:2.10: When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases.
translation_text.ud1.2:2.11: When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases.
translation_text.ud1.2:2.12: When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease.
translation_text.ud1.2:2.13: That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.”
translation_text.ud1.2:3.1: Then, understanding this matter, on that occasion the Buddha expressed this heartfelt sentiment:
translation_text.ud1.2:4.4: since he’s known the end of conditions.”
